region_of_origin: Global (widely used in Western and Southeast Asian baking)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wheat flour class; no dedicated self-raising flour subclass confirmed. Closest defensible parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers wheat flour broadly; self-raising variant with added leavening agents is not separately coded. Closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for self-raising wheat flour found in publicly accessible SNOMED CT browser. Set to null to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: blended with leavening agents (baking powder and salt)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-use serving of self-raising flour in home baking is approximately 30 g (about 3 level tablespoons), providing roughly 106 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: White wheat flour, self-raising — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for white wheat flour, self-raising (cr_id F05040016). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 20081 (Wheat flour, white, all-purpose, self-rising, enriched) for saturated fat and additional micronutrients. Where USDA values are used, this is noted per field.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for self-raising flour exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 71 is taken from the closest approved analogous food: white wheat flour (all-purpose), GI ~71 (glucose reference), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database. Self-raising flour differs only in the addition of baking powder and salt, which do not materially alter the glycaemic response of the starch fraction. GL per serving calculated as: (71 × 21.96 g available carbohydrate per 30 g serving) / 100 = 15.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 740 mg/100 g from added baking powder and salt — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit self-raising flour; substituting plain white flour with a small, measured amount of baking powder allows sodium to be controlled, and total daily sodium from all baked goods should be counted toward the recommended limit of under 2000 mg/day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined carbohydrate (73.2 g/100 g carbohydrate, GI ~71, dietary fibre only 2.2 g/100 g) producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should minimise use of self-raising flour; where baking is desired, substituting at least 50% with wholemeal or high-fibre flour, reducing portion size, and combining with protein and fat ingredients in the recipe will help moderate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (740 mg/100 g) raises blood pressure risk; refined carbohydrate with low fibre contributes to dyslipidaemia and elevated triglycerides with frequent consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, self-raising flour should be used sparingly; replacing it with wholegrain alternatives increases dietary fibre and reduces the glycaemic and sodium load, supporting better lipid profiles and blood pressure control.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (740 mg/100 g) exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA sodium threshold; refined carbohydrate promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid self-raising flour due to its high sodium content and pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate profile; wholemeal flour with minimal added baking powder is a preferable alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White wheat flour, self-raising, is a processed baking ingredient made from refined white wheat flour blended with chemical leavening agents — typically baking powder (a mixture of sodium bicarbonate and acid salts such as cream of tartar or sodium aluminium sulphate) and salt. The result is a fine, white, smooth powder that causes baked goods to rise without the need for separately added baking powder or yeast. It is widely used in cakes, scones, pancakes, muffins, and quick breads across Western and Southeast Asian home baking traditions. Per 100 g it provides 353 kcal, 10 g protein, 1.3 g fat, and 73.2 g carbohydrate with only 2.2 g dietary fibre, making it a high-glycaemic-index (GI ~71) refined carbohydrate. The most clinically significant feature is its sodium content of 740 mg/100 g — substantially higher than plain white flour (~2 mg/100 g) — due entirely to the added baking powder and salt. This makes it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, or cardiovascular disease without careful portion control. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the high GI and low fibre content promote rapid postprandial glucose elevation. Nutritionally, self-raising flour is enriched with B-vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, folate) and provides modest amounts of iron, calcium, potassium, and phosphorus. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable but contains gluten and is not appropriate for coeliac or gluten-intolerant individuals.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in self-raising flour?
What is the difference between self-raising flour and plain flour?
Can I use self-raising flour if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in self-raising flour?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05040016, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and water content cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 20081 (Wheat flour, white, all-purpose, self-rising, enriched), which is the closest approved global equivalent. GI estimated from the closest approved analogous food (white wheat flour, GI 71) per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.2 g), monounsaturated fat (0.1 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.5 g), trans fat (0.0 g confirmed trace), B-vitamins (thiamine 0.6 mg, riboflavin 0.4 mg, niacin 5.1 mg, folate 183 mcg DFE), phosphorus (97 mg), magnesium (20 mg), zinc (0.7 mg), and water (11.9 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 20081 as HPB does not report these fields for this item. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these values are not reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy entry 20081 for self-raising flour. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-derived flour product; cholesterol is naturally absent). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived product). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived product, not fortified per HPB record). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible per USDA SR Legacy). 4. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat flour (all-purpose) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008; self-raising flour has no direct GI entry in approved sources. The leavening agents do not materially alter starch glycaemic response. 5. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are closest_parent matches to the general wheat flour class; no dedicated self-raising flour subclass exists in either system as of the access date. SNOMED CT code set to null — no confirmed code found. 6. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; mandatory multilingual local-name fields are not applicable and are set to null per G6 rules.
